jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana brainstorming inventions, already at 59 ideas, treating this as serious genius work
jokes.setup: Two broke twenty-somethings sitting in a park listing inventions as if they're serious entrepreneurs
jokes.punchline: The sheer confidence and momentum of the list — they're at 59 already and treat each dumb idea as a breakthrough
jokes.explanation: Solid scene-setting joke. The premise that they're deeply committed to listing inventions they'll clearly never pursue, and that they're already at 59, efficiently establishes both characters' delusional self-confidence. Functional but not sharp.

jokes.text: 'GIF' pronunciation debate — 'I know, but I am not doing that.' / 'No, it's disgusting.'
jokes.setup: Someone at work told Abbi it's pronounced 'jiff'
jokes.punchline: Both characters react with visceral disgust and total refusal, treating a minor pronunciation dispute as a moral stance
jokes.explanation: The joke works because of how economically it's delivered — the disgust is completely disproportionate to the stakes. 'No, it's disgusting' as a response to a pronunciation preference is a perfect small character beat. Very relatable in 2016 and delivered with great economy.

jokes.text: 'A gynecologist that's also a bikini waxer.' / 'That is a literal one-stop pussy shop.'
jokes.setup: Abbi pitches the gynecologist/bikini waxer combo as an invention idea
jokes.punchline: Ilana summarizes it with the phrase 'one-stop pussy shop' with complete enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'one-stop pussy shop' is highly quotable and captures Ilana's voice perfectly — unfiltered, efficient, and proud. The setup is Abbi's practical-minded pitch; the punchline is Ilana's reductive but accurate branding. Good character differentiation. Nearly an 8 on craft but held back slightly by the vulgarity doing some of the work.

jokes.text: 'I think we might actually be literal geniuses.' / 'Oh, we for sure are geniuses.'
jokes.setup: The list of inventions so far includes a GIF basket app and a gynecologist/bikini waxer
jokes.punchline: They both sincerely believe they're geniuses based on this output
jokes.explanation: Functional escalation of the scene's premise. The irony is clear and well-placed but not particularly surprising given the setup. The 'for sure' is a nice Ilana touch — the upgrade from 'might be' to 'for sure' in one exchange is a small but effective beat.

jokes.text: 'A skateboard, with a handle.' / 'That's, like, a scooter.'
jokes.setup: Ilana pitches her invention with the same confident energy as all the others
jokes.punchline: Abbi flatly informs her this already exists as a scooter
jokes.explanation: Textbook economy joke. Ilana delivers the 'invention' with full confidence; Abbi's one-word deflation ('That's, like, a scooter') is perfectly timed. The 'Derr' follow-up where Ilana mocks herself escalates it nicely. The gap between Ilana's confidence and the mundanity of the idea is the engine here.

jokes.text: 'Derr. Derr, derr, derr, derr.' — Ilana mocking herself after the scooter revelation
jokes.setup: Abbi just told her a skateboard with a handle is a scooter
jokes.punchline: Ilana's self-mocking 'derr' sound effect, repeated with relish — she's delighted by her own stupidity
jokes.explanation: A small but effective character moment. Ilana's self-delighted stupidity is a signature trait and she leans into it here. The repetition of 'derr' is physical comedy in audio form. Not groundbreaking but very Ilana.

jokes.text: Magnetic boots that pick up change — 'Then I could be the Jewish stereotype that I am without being known for it.'
jokes.setup: Abbi pitches magnetic boots so you never have to bend over to pick up change
jokes.punchline: The motivation is to secretly fulfill a Jewish money stereotype without anyone knowing
jokes.explanation: The joke earns its subversiveness because it's self-directed and the logic is airtight — the invention serves as a workaround to live out a stereotype covertly. The specificity ('without being known for it') is what makes it land. Good originality for a quick throwaway.

jokes.text: 'Swimmers have, like, hard pubes and shriveled dicks.'
jokes.setup: They're apparently ranking athletes by attractiveness in a brunch conversation
jokes.punchline: Ilana's unprompted, authoritative dismissal of swimmers based on chlorine-related genital specifics
jokes.explanation: The joke is almost entirely character-driven — this line could only be Ilana. The specificity ('hard pubes') is what elevates it from a generic dirty joke to something truly weird and memorable. Delivered with complete conviction, which is the key. Strong quotability.

jokes.text: 'For me, it's wrestlers, gymnasts... jockeys, bay-bay! I would ride those tiny jacked little bodies. It's like me, but, like, a boy.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is ranking athletes by sexual attractiveness
jokes.punchline: The escalating specificity of 'jockeys' as a sexual preference, followed by 'it's like me, but like, a boy' — she's attracted to a male version of herself
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The build — wrestlers, gymnasts, then the unexpected pivot to jockeys — is great structure. But the real punchline is the self-revelation: she wants a 'tiny jacked' male version of herself. It's absurdist, deeply character-specific, and the self-love/narcissism angle is a perfect Ilana character note. 'Bay-bay' as the exclamation sells it. Earns the 8.

jokes.text: DMV buildup: 'V, V, V...' — Ilana can't even say the word
jokes.setup: Ilana has just told Abbi she has to get her license renewed
jokes.punchline: Ilana trails off saying just the letters 'V, V, V' as if 'DMV' is too horrible to speak aloud
jokes.explanation: A small, well-timed beat. The refusal to complete the acronym treats the DMV as a Voldemort-level horror. Works as quick character comedy without overstaying its welcome.

jokes.text: 'In the span of your whole lifetime, it's a second. But in reality, it'll be, like, 12 hours.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is reassuring Abbi that the DMV won't be that bad
jokes.punchline: She immediately undercuts the reassurance with a realistic 12-hour estimate
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection structure — the first clause sets up conventional reassurance, the second destroys it with brutal honesty. Clean, well-executed. Universal DMV relatability helps the impact. The two-clause structure is textbook.

jokes.text: Abbi announces she'll finally take a good license photo — 'This is gonna be the time that I take a license photo that is good.'
jokes.setup: Standard optimism about an impossible task
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'that is good' phrasing — her bar is so low it implies a lifetime of photo disasters
jokes.explanation: Functional setup for the episode's A-plot. The joke is mostly setup work — it establishes the stakes of Abbi's mission. Not particularly sharp on its own but necessary scaffolding.

jokes.text: 'With that ass?' / 'They don't let you put your ass in the photo.' / 'They really should.'
jokes.setup: Ilana enthusiastically supports Abbi getting a good photo
jokes.punchline: Ilana's encouragement is entirely based on Abbi's ass, which is not photographed on a license
jokes.explanation: Three-line exchange with clean escalation. 'With that ass?' is the non-sequitur setup, 'They don't let you' is the deflation, 'They really should' is Ilana doubling down on her ass-centric worldview. Very much Ilana's character — her admiration for Abbi's body is a recurring show element delivered with genuine warmth here.

jokes.text: 'I know you from your ass better than I know your face.' / 'You know me from my ass better than you know me from my face?' / 'Yep, and hearing it said back to me slower only validates it more.'
jokes.setup: Ilana makes the intimate admission that she identifies Abbi primarily by her butt
jokes.punchline: Abbi's incredulous repetition and Ilana's meta-confirmation that hearing it back only makes it more true
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best exchanges. The structure is excellent: absurd claim → incredulous repetition → meta-validation. The final line ('hearing it said back to me slower only validates it more') is the key — it's a joke ABOUT how jokes work, but grounded in character. Ilana's unshakeable confidence in her own weird logic is the show's core comic engine, and this exemplifies it. Quotable, character-specific, well-paced. Earns the 8.

jokes.text: 'I applied for over three jobs and haven't gotten any. Today is all about that hustle.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is framing her day as a serious job-seeking hustle
jokes.punchline: 'Over three' — the bar for hustle is hilariously low; three applications is her entire effort
jokes.explanation: 'Over three' is doing a lot of work — it implies she barely cleared three, and uses 'over' to make it sound bigger than it is. The self-delusion is perfect Ilana. Economy is high; the joke lands in five words. Delivered straight to someone off-camera (a talking-head confessional beat).

jokes.text: Ilana's lip color: 'I found it, it was a rescue. There was more than half left, so I cut the top off.'
jokes.setup: Someone compliments Ilana's lip color; she's asked where she got it
jokes.punchline: She found it on the ground, calls it a 'rescue,' and reveals she saws the top off used lipstick — broke girl logic delivered with zero shame
jokes.explanation: The 'rescue' framing is the key — applying animal rescue language to found lipstick is a perfectly Ilana move. 'There was more than half left' as her quality threshold is a great specific detail. Abbi's horrified 'You don't... buy new. Do you?' is the perfect straight-woman reaction. High character integration.

jokes.text: Hair stylist promises Abbi she'll look 'just like a Bachelor contestant' — 'Oh, uh, no...' / 'No, but one that quits 'cause she's too good for the show.'
jokes.setup: Hairdresser offers a 'Bachelor contestant' look as a compliment
jokes.punchline: Abbi's horrified reaction is immediately smoothed over with the specific caveat 'one that quits 'cause she's too good for the show'
jokes.explanation: The misdirection is clean: 'Bachelor contestant' as a beauty compliment is understandably alarming; the qualifier rescues it with a very specific, flattering revision. The specificity of 'quits because she's too good' is what makes it work — it's not just walking it back, it's upgrading the image.

jokes.text: Abbi's rogues' gallery of bad professional photos — license, senior photo with braces, homecoming photo
jokes.setup: Abbi claims all her professional photos are bad; hairdresser says everyone says that
jokes.punchline: Abbi has them all on her phone and proceeds to show a series of increasingly horrifying photos
jokes.explanation: Standard escalation visual gag — each photo is worse than the last. Works well on screen where we can see the photos. Transcript-only limits some of the humor here since the visual content is the point. Functions as setup for the gay boyfriend and bee jokes within it.

jokes.text: The gay high school boyfriend photo: 'You think he was gay?' / 'Um, I don't like to go off of looks, but I mean...' / 'He was gay.' / 'Yeah, he was gay.'
jokes.setup: Abbi shows her homecoming photo with her high school boyfriend; hairdresser notices something
jokes.punchline: Both agree he was obviously gay, with the escalating revelation that 'everybody knew' including all the teachers — but Abbi genuinely didn't
jokes.explanation: The joke escalates nicely — from visual suggestion, to confirmation, to 'everybody knew,' to 'the teachers knew too.' Abbi's obliviousness is the engine. The hairdresser's 'I don't like to go off of looks, but I mean...' is a good diplomatic pause before the obvious conclusion. The 'billionaire now in Idaho' tag is a bonus beat.

jokes.text: 'And he's actually a billionaire now. In Idaho, yeah.'
jokes.setup: Just established he was gay and everyone knew it
jokes.punchline: He's a billionaire now — in Idaho specifically, which is a funny detail that implies a very specific kind of gay Idaho billionaire life
jokes.explanation: The 'Idaho' specificity is doing a lot of work — it's funnier than any other state would be. The non-sequitur wealth revelation (everyone knew he was gay and he turned out fine — great, actually) is a good subversion of what could have been a mean joke. The geography makes it weird and memorable.

jokes.text: The passport photo has a bee in it — 'This bee followed me around Astoria for 30 days.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is showing her terrible photos; this is the passport
jokes.punchline: There is a bee in the passport photo — and Abbi's explanation is that a specific bee followed her for a full month
jokes.explanation: Genuinely original and absurdist. A bee in a passport photo is funny on its own; the explanation that this specific bee haunted her for 30 days transforms it from a funny visual to a full absurdist mythology. 'This bee followed me around Astoria for 30 days' delivered as a matter-of-fact explanation is pitch perfect. High originality.

jokes.text: The Deals, Deals, Deals coupon makes the haircut $15 — and the hairdresser visibly deflates and does a rushed, minimal cut
jokes.setup: Abbi has been building to this specific haircut for her DMV photo; she pulls out a discount coupon at the last minute
jokes.punchline: The hairdresser's enthusiasm evaporates; she does an immediate bare-minimum cut and declares 'we are done'
jokes.explanation: Well-structured setup and payoff. The Deals, Deals, Deals coupon (introduced earlier by Ilana) pays off here. The hairdresser's instantaneous pivot from warmth to minimum-viable service is the punchline. 'And we are done' / 'Are we?' / 'Yeah.' is a perfect three-line exchange. Good callback structure.

jokes.text: 'Ow, my subluxation!' — Abbi immediately injures her neck after the haircut
jokes.setup: Abbi just got a terrible rushed haircut; she stands up
jokes.punchline: She immediately injures her neck with a subluxation — setting up the chiropractor B-plot
jokes.explanation: The word 'subluxation' is doing some heavy lifting — it's funnier than just 'neck' because it's medical and specific. Primarily a plot mechanism joke but the exclamation is delivered with enough commitment that it earns a laugh. Sets up later payoffs.

jokes.text: Ilana's subway performance speech: 'I've fallen on hard times. After graduating from NYU, I lost my job at an Internet startup. Deals, Deals, Deals. Check 'em out, I bear them no ill will.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is performing on the subway for tips, giving a context-setting speech
jokes.punchline: She specifies the startup by name and adds 'I bear them no ill will' — the weirdly formal magnanimity toward her former employer while panhandling on the subway
jokes.explanation: The callback to Deals, Deals, Deals is well-deployed. 'I bear them no ill will' is a beautiful phrase in this context — it's the kind of formal dignity that's absurd when you're busking on the A train. Ilana's brand of self-aware hustle is perfectly captured here. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Ilana performs on the subway — the act itself (inferred: some kind of flip or stunt) earns actual money from commuters
jokes.setup: Ilana announces she's 'performing for tips' with no presh
jokes.punchline: She appears to successfully earn tips — the comedy is that her subway hustle actually works
jokes.explanation: Primarily a visual gag that doesn't translate well to transcript. The 'no presh' casualness is good, and we get context from Eliot's later comment about filming it for his Tumblr. The fact that she succeeds at this absurd hustle is the joke — but it lives on screen.

jokes.text: Eliot reveals he was filming Ilana for his Tumblr: 'Q Believe This Idiot. Tumblr.com'
jokes.setup: Ilana is excited that her brother is filming her, thinking it's admiration
jokes.punchline: He's filming her for a Tumblr called 'Q Believe This Idiot' — he thinks she's a public embarrassment
jokes.explanation: 'Q Believe This Idiot' is a great specific joke — the name is perfectly calibrated humiliation. Ilana's immediate pivot to 'Oh, my God. Me? ...Here, get a better one?' (offering herself up enthusiastically) is brilliant follow-through — she interprets the humiliation as fame. Very Ilana.

jokes.text: Ilana offers to 'get a better one' for Eliot's humiliation Tumblr
jokes.setup: Eliot just revealed he's been filming her for 'Q Believe This Idiot'
jokes.punchline: Ilana's response is to offer to perform again for a better shot — she has no shame and interprets documentation as celebrity
jokes.explanation: This reaction is perfect Ilana — she's incapable of shame and reframes every negative as positive attention. The joke is the reaction, not a line, and it's deeply character-integrated. Ilana offering to help her brother document her own idiocy more clearly is a perfect absurdist character beat. Earns the 8 on character integration alone.

jokes.text: 'Mom doesn't know you got fired and I'm getting tired of covering for you. I'm sure you've got a Flappy Bird in you.'
jokes.setup: Eliot is admonishing Ilana to get a real job
jokes.punchline: 'I'm sure you've got a Flappy Bird in you' — a completely sincere and extremely low-bar compliment suggesting she can make one viral app
jokes.explanation: The 'Flappy Bird' reference works specifically because it implies Eliot's bar for Ilana's potential is a single accidental viral hit made by one person in a weekend. It's a very specific 2016 cultural touchstone that ages the joke but also perfectly calibrates the low expectations. Good Eliot-as-exasperated-brother character note.

jokes.text: 'You not listening to me about this is homophobic.' / 'It is?' / 'No, it's not, but get a job.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is defending subway performing as a 'real job'
jokes.punchline: She deploys 'homophobic' as a rhetorical weapon, then immediately admits it doesn't apply — but the key is she knew it didn't work and used it anyway
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structure is elegant: false accusation → straight man's alarmed 'It is?' → immediate self-correction. But what makes it exceptional is Ilana's self-awareness — she KNOWS it's not homophobic, she just deployed it as a conversational weapon and immediately abandoned it. It's both a character joke and a commentary on how identity-based accusations get weaponized. Three lines, maximum information. Highly quotable. Earns the 8.

jokes.text: 'I knew you were right before I did the flip.'
jokes.setup: Ilana and Eliot have been arguing about whether subway performing is a real job
jokes.punchline: Ilana admits retrospectively she knew she was wrong before she did the risky flip — but did it anyway
jokes.explanation: Short, punchy, and highly character-specific. The idea that she possessed the self-awareness to know it was a bad idea but did the flip anyway is a perfect Ilana thesis statement. 'Before I did the flip' implies she went ahead despite knowing — which is exactly who she is. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Abbi sees the DMV and thinks 'that's not so bad' — then the door opens to reveal the full nightmare interior
jokes.setup: Abbi approaches the DMV with cautious optimism
jokes.punchline: Brief false comfort before the full horror is revealed
jokes.explanation: Standard misdirection beat — the 'not so bad' / actual horror structure is well-worn. Functions well as a scene transition joke but has no particular originality. The comedy is entirely in the visual reveal which we can't assess from transcript.

jokes.text: Abbi gets called number G-42 — 'Picture of a hammer?'
jokes.setup: Standard DMV number calling
jokes.punchline: Abbi's ticket apparently has a picture of a hammer on it instead of a normal number — she's confused by the system
jokes.explanation: A small, well-placed absurdist beat. The DMV having an inexplicable pictographic system is a very specific comedic idea. Abbi's confused 'Picture of a hammer?' is a good line because it's specific — not a star or a circle, a HAMMER.

jokes.text: Abbi is called almost immediately at the DMV — she's shocked and overjoyed: 'I'm free!'
jokes.setup: The entire episode has built up the DMV as a 12-hour nightmare
jokes.punchline: She gets called almost immediately — the wait is nothing
jokes.explanation: Solid misdirection payoff on the DMV dread built up earlier. The setup was extensive — the DMV has been framed as a life-consuming ordeal — so the immediate call is a good subversion. 'I'm free!' is delivered with an intensity that exceeds the situation, which is the joke.

jokes.text: Ilana begging the temp agency: 'Come on... you're a temp agency. Look, I've asked you three times. Just please leave.' / 'I can't, I need a job.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is at a temp agency trying to get work
jokes.punchline: She's been asked to leave three times and is refusing — her need is genuine but her awareness of social norms is absent
jokes.explanation: Good cringe beat. Ilana refusing to leave a job agency sets up the backstory reveal about why she's been blacklisted. The 'I can't, I need a job' is delivered with genuine desperation, which makes it funnier — she knows she should leave but the stakes are real.

jokes.text: The manager lists why she can't help Ilana: 'The Chinese food? I was hungry. The plane tickets? I was thirsty. The door? I did not know it was a door.'
jokes.setup: The manager explains that every time she tries to help Ilana, it becomes a disaster
jokes.punchline: Ilana's three explanations escalate perfectly: hunger → thirst (wrong word) → didn't know it was a door
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. The escalating three-part structure is perfect: Chinese food/hungry (plausible) → plane tickets/thirsty (wrong excuse, wrong crime) → 'I did not know it was a door' (incomprehensible, absurdist, raises infinite questions). Each one is funnier than the last and the logic deteriorates beautifully. 'I was thirsty' as the explanation for stealing plane tickets is genius — she just swapped in the wrong body need. And 'I did not know it was a door' is a perfect comedy ending because it explains nothing while implying everything. Economy is nearly perfect. Earns a 9.

jokes.text: Agency manager reveals her life is falling apart: 'Between you and me... I'm barely hanging on to this business. No. See this tooth? Dead.'
jokes.setup: The manager is trying to explain why she can't take on Ilana as a problem right now
jokes.punchline: She overshares that she's barely keeping the business alive, then — as evidence — points to a dead tooth
jokes.explanation: The dead tooth as a symbol of life falling apart is a very specific and visually effective comedic choice. 'Dead' delivered as a flat, definitive summary of both the tooth and her situation is good deadpan. The oversharing pivot from professional to personal is well-handled.

jokes.text: 'Is it supposed to take more than three days to heat up a potato?' / 'I don't think so.' / 'See? Everything in my life is broken.'
jokes.setup: The manager is cataloguing her life falling apart
jokes.punchline: Her evidence for everything being broken is a potato that's been heating for three days
jokes.explanation: The specificity is what makes this great — not 'my food won't cook' but 'a potato, three days.' It raises so many questions (what appliance? why a potato? how has she been eating?) and Ilana's 'I don't think so' delivered as sincere uncertainty is the perfect straight-man response. The potato functions as a perfect comedy object: unglamorous, specific, absurd. High originality.

jokes.text: 'Plus, I'm two months pregnant.' / 'Two?!' / 'Yeah.' / 'I probably shouldn't even be telling people yet.'
jokes.setup: The manager is already oversharing about her life falling apart
jokes.punchline: The pregnancy revelation is delivered as another item on the list of disasters, then she realizes she shouldn't be sharing this with a stranger
jokes.explanation: Ilana's 'Two?!' is funny because her concern is the number of months, not the pregnancy itself. The manager immediately regretting the overshare ('I probably shouldn't even be telling people yet') is good cringe comedy — she shared it anyway while knowing she shouldn't.

jokes.text: Ilana ends up lying on the floor comforting the manager: 'Will you just come lay with me? Just... hold my hand?'
jokes.setup: This was supposed to be a job inquiry; instead the manager has broken down
jokes.punchline: The manager is now asking Ilana — who came to ask for work — to lie on the floor and hold her hand
jokes.explanation: The complete role reversal — Ilana came to ask for help and ends up being asked to provide emotional support — is well-executed. The floor-lying is a visual escalation of the manager's breakdown. The whole sequence builds a good miniature cringe character out of a minor supporting role.

jokes.text: 'Maybe I should just chuck it all and start over.' / 'Do you know of any good jobs?' / 'No...' / 'Do you?'
jokes.setup: They're lying on the floor together; the manager is contemplating life changes
jokes.punchline: The manager turns to Ilana — the person who came in looking for a job — for job advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. The exchange has four short lines but contains a complete ironic inversion — the job agency is asking the unemployed person for job leads. The silence implied between 'No...' and 'Do you?' is where the joke lives. Very sharp little capper to the scene.

jokes.text: The DMV worker offers Abbi a snack mid-photo session
jokes.setup: Abbi is trying to get her photo taken at the DMV
jokes.punchline: The DMV worker casually offers her something to snack on — normalizing the DMV as a welcoming social space
jokes.explanation: Light absurdist beat — the DMV is being presented as bizarrely pleasant and social. The snack offering is a small but effective humanizing-of-the-DMV joke. Context-dependent; hard to fully assess without the visual.

jokes.text: Abbi's photo attempt fails — she twists her already-injured neck trying to pose
jokes.setup: Abbi has been setting up for the perfect photo all episode; her neck is already injured
jokes.punchline: She injures herself further trying to get the perfect shot — she can't even face forward properly
jokes.explanation: Good structural callback — the neck injury established earlier pays off at the exact moment of the photo. 'Can't we just do, like, a sideways, like, pouty, cool thing?' against the regulation requirement to face forward is a nice specific detail. The physical comedy presumably plays well on screen.

jokes.text: DMV number being called — Abbi's intense celebration: 'That's my number! There's mine! I win! Oh, excuse me.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has been waiting at the DMV, presumably for hours
jokes.punchline: 'I win!' — framing a DMV number being called as a competitive victory, followed by the social grace of 'Oh, excuse me'
jokes.explanation: The 'I win!' as DMV framing is good character comedy — Abbi's relief expressed as triumph. The immediate social correction ('Oh, excuse me') is a nice contrast beat that shows she's still fundamentally rule-abiding even in her moment of DMV glory.

jokes.text: DMV worker makes Abbi repeat: 'I am a badass queen.' / 'Kyuh, kyuh, kyuh!'
jokes.setup: The DMV worker is trying to get Abbi to take a good photo
jokes.punchline: The motivational protocol involves repeating affirmations and making strange sounds — extremely non-regulation DMV behavior
jokes.explanation: Works as part of the 'surprisingly wonderful DMV' running premise. The affirmation ritual with the 'kyuh kyuh kyuh' vocal warmup is a specific absurdist detail. The DMV worker as an unlikely life coach is the gag's engine.

jokes.text: 'I have the perfect job for you.' / 'Social worker?' / 'Bike messenger.' / 'Even better.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has been calling the DMV worker 'very wise' with 'so much insight'
jokes.punchline: The DMV worker recommends bike messenger as the perfect job — not a caring profession but a physically demanding delivery gig
jokes.explanation: Clean two-beat misdirection. 'Social worker?' sets up the expected heartwarming conclusion; 'Bike messenger' is the deflating reality. Abbi's immediate 'even better' acceptance is a good character beat — she's so swept up in the moment she accepts this as wisdom.

jokes.text: 'Ass, ass, ass, ass.' — Ilana chanting as Abbi begins bike messengering
jokes.setup: Abbi is riding away as a new bike messenger
jokes.punchline: Ilana's send-off is a pure rear-end appreciation chant — callbacks to the entire ass-centric conversation earlier
jokes.explanation: Four-word callback to the entire ass-focused conversation earlier. Ilana's devotion to Abbi's physical form is one of the show's consistent threads, and 'ass, ass, ass, ass' as a simple chant is a good Ilana vocalization. Economy score is very high — maximum reference density.

jokes.text: First package delivery — Ilana throws it to the person across the street, who catches it: 'This isn't for me!' / 'Oh, yes it is!' / 'How did she do that?'
jokes.setup: Ilana has her first delivery as a bike messenger
jokes.punchline: She throws the package from her bike without stopping; the recipient protests it's not theirs; Ilana insists it is; bystander is amazed by the throw
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy beat where the joke is Ilana's total confidence in her wrong delivery. 'Oh, yes it is!' delivered with complete certainty while wrong is good Ilana character work. The bystander's genuine amazement at the athletic throw adds a nice third layer.

jokes.text: 'My ass is v. sore, but that's what you get when you're BM queen. I mean it both ways.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is updating Abbi on her new bike messenger career
jokes.punchline: 'BM' stands for both Bike Messenger and bowel movement; Ilana is aware of both meanings and embraces both
jokes.explanation: 'I mean it both ways' is the key delivery — the pun is obvious but Ilana's deliberate embrace of the scatological meaning is what makes it Broad City rather than a dad joke. Sore ass from biking + proud bowel movement queen is a very Ilana combination.

jokes.text: Abbi riding on Ilana's bike messenger bike, Ilana singing: '♪ There she is / The most beautiful girl in the world ♪' while apparently crashing immediately
jokes.setup: Ilana promised Abbi she 'won't even know she's on a bike'; Abbi immediately knows she's on a bike
jokes.punchline: Ilana sings a beauty pageant song while apparently unable to control the bike
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy built on the contrast between Ilana's romantic confidence and the immediate reality of danger. 'You won't even know you're on a bike' → 'I'm definitely on a bike!' is a clean setup/payoff. The beauty pageant song adds an absurdist layer.

jokes.text: 'I mostly tussle, but I think I'm getting, like, a solid six hours of tussling per night.'
jokes.setup: The chiropractor asks if Abbi is getting enough sleep
jokes.punchline: Abbi reframes her insomnia/poor sleep as 'tussling' — she doesn't sleep, she tussles, and quantifies this as adequate rest
jokes.explanation: The word 'tussle' doing double duty as a sleep euphemism is a highly specific and original choice. 'Solid six hours of tussling' as self-reassurance while describing what sounds like anxious restless nights is painfully relatable and linguistically precise. 'Tussle' is an underused word that works perfectly here. High quotability.

jokes.text: Someone bought one of Abbi's drawings off the chiropractor's wall for $45 — which exactly covers her co-pay, making the appointment free
jokes.setup: Abbi is a struggling artist whose work is displayed in her chiropractor's office
jokes.punchline: The sale of her art for exactly $45 (the co-pay amount) is the universe providing just enough and no more
jokes.explanation: Good broke-artist joke. The precision of $45 covering exactly the co-pay is a very specific version of the 'universe gives you just enough to break even' comedy beat. Abbi's genuine joy at this tiny financial win is endearing and funny.

jokes.text: 'And next time, when you make an appointment at the DMV, do it online. That way when you get there, it just takes a minute.' — Abbi re-injures her neck in shock
jokes.setup: The entire episode has treated the DMV as an unavoidable horror show
jokes.punchline: The chiropractor casually reveals you can make online appointments — which Abbi didn't know — and her shock re-injures her neck
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best structural payoffs. The entire DMV plot — the dread, the 12-hour estimate, the ordeal — was predicated on Abbi not knowing online appointments exist. The title of the episode ('2016') is the chiropractor's punchline ('This is 2016'). The re-injury is perfect physical comedy timing. This joke retroactively reframes the entire A-plot. Strong craft score.

jokes.text: 'Well, I don't know the Internet that well.'
jokes.setup: The chiropractor is baffled that Abbi doesn't know you can book the DMV online
jokes.punchline: Abbi's defense is that she doesn't know the internet well — in 2016, this is an absurd admission
jokes.explanation: Seven words that perfectly capture Abbi's character — smart enough to be embarrassed, not enough to have Googled it. 'The Internet' with the article is the key — it implies she relates to the internet as a foreign entity rather than a tool she uses. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: Dr. Heller books Abbi a DMV appointment on her phone in real time: 'Look at that, 4:45, you have one last appointment today. You can still make it.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just learned online DMV booking exists
jokes.punchline: The chiropractor immediately books it for her in the appointment, demonstrating just how easy it was this whole time
jokes.explanation: The speed and ease of the booking — done in seconds during a chiro appointment — is the punchline to the whole morning's ordeal. Works as escalation of the reveal joke.

jokes.text: 'Thanks, Dr. Heller. Love you.' / 'I mean, I'll see you at our next appointment.' / 'Hey, I love you, too.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is leaving the chiropractor's office excited
jokes.punchline: She says 'love you' casually; the doctor professionally corrects with 'I'll see you at our next appointment'; then immediately softens and reciprocates
jokes.explanation: A small three-beat cringe joke about professional boundaries. The doctor's initial correction is the setup; her immediate abandonment of that position ('Hey, I love you, too') is the punchline. The warmth of the relationship makes it sweet-funny rather than just awkward.

jokes.text: Ilana asks if they're hiring at Hillary Clinton's campaign HQ — immediately gets a job after showing her resumé on her photo stream
jokes.setup: Ilana arrived to deliver a package; she asks about jobs
jokes.punchline: She gets hired on the spot — and has her resumé saved in her photo stream 'for cases like this'
jokes.explanation: 'Saved on my photo stream for cases like this' is a perfect Ilana detail — the chaotic job-seeker who has apparently done this before enough times to develop a protocol. The casual way she presents a photo stream resumé is good character comedy. The 'just don't swipe right' line adds a nice capper.

jokes.text: 'Are you guys hiring?' 'Do you have a CV?' 'A... coconut vice?' 'A resumé?' 'Oh. Um, it is saved on my photo stream for cases like this.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is now trying to get a job at Clinton HQ after delivering a package
jokes.punchline: She doesn't know what a CV is ('coconut vice') and has her resumé saved in her camera roll as a standard job-hunting protocol
jokes.explanation: 'Coconut vice' as a guess at CV is a great malapropism — wrong, but the sounds aren't far off, and the explanation shows Ilana genuinely tried to parse it. The resumé-in-photo-stream detail is specifically 2016-relatable and shows someone who has adapted job-hunting to their actual technological habits. 'For cases like this' implies she does this regularly.

jokes.text: 'Just don't swipe right.' / 'I just accidentally swiped right.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is showing her resumé in her photo stream
jokes.punchline: She warns not to swipe right (into personal photos); the staffer immediately swipes right
jokes.explanation: Classic 'don't press the red button' structure — warning immediately violated. Works well because what's to the right of the resumé in Ilana's photo stream is presumably very NSFW. Two lines, perfectly efficient. The implication is funnier than any explanation.

jokes.text: 'You should probably see a doctor about that rash.' / 'I know what it is, I'm just tracking the progress, thank you so much.'
jokes.setup: The staffer has seen something in Ilana's photo stream after swiping right
jokes.punchline: Ilana has a documented rash she's photographically monitoring rather than treating
jokes.explanation: 'I'm just tracking the progress' is a genius response — it implies Ilana has a whole scientific attitude toward her own untreated skin condition. The 'thank you so much' at the end, as if dismissing medical advice while appreciating the concern, is perfect character delivery. High quotability; the logic is Ilana at her best.

jokes.text: 'Ilana Wexler and Hillary Clinton? Two powerful whomen whorking as whone?'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just been given a volunteer position at the Clinton campaign
jokes.punchline: She imagines herself as Hillary's equal partner — and the 'wh' spelling/pronunciation of 'women/working/one' implies a feminist intensity to the vowel sounds
jokes.explanation: 'Whomen whorking as whone' is a delightful specific vocal affectation — Ilana apparently pronounces 'women/working/one' with an exaggerated 'wh' sound when she's feeling empowered. The grandiosity of placing herself as Hillary's equal partner is classic Ilana self-mythology. Highly quotable and distinctly Broad City.

jokes.text: Campaign staffer's list of things not to answer questions about: 'Make-up. Bras. Panties.'
jokes.setup: New volunteers are being briefed on cold-calling protocol
jokes.punchline: The list of off-limit topics reveals the specific sexist questions female politicians get from voters
jokes.explanation: Efficient political commentary delivered as comedy. The escalation from 'make-up' (somewhat expected) to 'bras' to 'panties' builds to an absurd and specifically gendered-harassment reveal. The joke is funnier AND sharper because it's true. Good economy — three words do a lot.

jokes.text: Hillary Clinton has 'won a Grammy' listed alongside 'Secretary of State' and 'expanded health insurance for lower income families.'
jokes.setup: Staff is being briefed on Hillary's credentials for cold-calling.
jokes.punchline: The Grammy is a real fact (she won for spoken word audiobooks) but dropped among major policy achievements as an equal credential, making it sound absurd.
jokes.explanation: The joke works because the Grammy is real — which makes it funnier than a made-up credential. Listing it alongside healthcare expansion creates perfect comic bathos.

jokes.text: 'No, Hillary does not cry at the office.' / 'Yes, Hillary can read a map.' / 'No, Hillary will not enforce male birth control or male pregnancy, as that is not a thing.' / 'And no, Hillary is not a witch.'
jokes.setup: The staffer is giving volunteers answers to common questions they receive on cold calls
jokes.punchline: The questions people actually ask about Hillary escalate from gendered to absurd to witch accusation
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation structure — starts with real sexism (crying, map-reading), pivots to absurd projection (male pregnancy enforcement), lands on medieval superstition (witch). The four-beat escalation is well-constructed and the comedy is genuine satire. 'As that is not a thing' is a great deadpan qualifier. 'Is it a witch?' gets a big reaction. Strong earned comedy — the humor comes from real gendered political discourse.

jokes.text: 'Do people seriously ask this stuff?' / 'Every day.' / 'Every day!'
jokes.setup: The list of questions about Hillary just ended
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'every day!' echoes the staffer's confirmation — they're horrified together, but Ilana's echo adds extra dramatism
jokes.explanation: A reaction beat that extends the previous joke's laugh. Ilana's repeated 'Every day!' is outrage comedy — she's newly horrified by misogyny, which is funny given how unshockable she usually is. Functional tag to a stronger joke.

jokes.text: Ilana arrives at the dreamlike DMV waiting area — chocolate fountain, edible flowers, massage, matching strawberries and carpet
jokes.setup: The episode has treated the DMV as a nightmare; Abbi booked her online appointment and arrives
jokes.punchline: The online-appointment DMV is a luxury spa-like wonderland with chocolate fountains and personalized service
jokes.explanation: The payoff for the entire DMV premise. The online-DMV as utopian luxury is a great comic concept — the mundane government office transformed into something approaching a fancy hotel, but specifically because of internet booking. The detail about strawberries matching the carpet suggests insane attention to aesthetic. Good visual comedy that requires the screen. Strong impact.

jokes.text: 'May I say, what gorgeous hair you're gracing us with today?' / 'My, uh... My chiropractor did it.' / 'Oh, you're hilarious. I daresay you're the funniest person I've ever met.'
jokes.setup: The DMV staff is complimenting Abbi
jokes.punchline: Abbi's embarrassing admission that her chiropractor did her hair — meant as an explanation, taken as a witty quip
jokes.explanation: The callback layers work well here — 'chiropractor did my hair' is an absurd truth that gets interpreted as a charming joke. The DMV staff's over-the-top enthusiasm ('funniest person I've ever met') contrasts with Abbi's genuine confusion. Good character moment for Abbi.

jokes.text: 'Pweeze, Mr. Cwusty Old White Man, can I pweeze keep my ovawies?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is on a cold call helping a woman figure out where to vote
jokes.punchline: We hear only Ilana's side — apparently she's been ranting about reproductive rights using a baby voice to mock the paternalistic political system
jokes.explanation: The baby-voice political commentary ('Cwusty Old White Man') is a perfect Ilana move — she's doing what was asked (confirming voting locations) but can't resist editorializing. 'Can I pweeze keep my ovawies?' delivered in an infantilizing voice to mock the infantilizing of women's healthcare is sharp satire in Ilana's specific register. High character integration and quotability.

jokes.text: Campaign manager asks if that was a personal call. 'No. Uh, this woman was bovo'ing out 'cause she didn't know where to vote and I was like, I got you.'
jokes.setup: The staffer clearly heard the 'Cwusty Old White Man' speech on what was supposed to be a voter information call
jokes.punchline: Ilana reframes her rant as helpful voter information work — 'bovo'ing out' as a made-up term for panicking
jokes.explanation: 'Bovo'ing out' is a made-up Ilana slang term that's very show-specific. The 'I was like, I got you' self-assessment of her rant as helpful is solid irony. Her complete confidence that the ovaries speech was on-mission is good character work.

jokes.text: 'Hi, I'm calling on behalf of Hillary Rodham Clinton.' / 'No, she's not a witch.' / 'Um... Do I sound like her?'
jokes.setup: Ilana starts a new cold call
jokes.punchline: She immediately gets the witch question and then seems to consider whether she sounds like Hillary Clinton
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback deployment — the witch question from the briefing immediately comes true on her first call. The 'Do I sound like her?' beat implies someone asked if she was Hillary on the phone — a great absurdist follow-through. Very economical: three lines cover a lot of comedic ground.

jokes.text: The DMV photo is taken — Abbi is thrilled, DMV worker confirms 'we got it,' then offers to do one more 'for me'
jokes.setup: The entire episode has been building to Abbi getting a good license photo
jokes.punchline: 'Let's do one more, for me' — the DMV worker has become so invested in Abbi that she wants a personal keepsake
jokes.explanation: 'For me' is the key phrase — it implies the DMV worker is keeping a copy for personal reasons, blurring the professional/personal boundary in the nicest possible way. The whole luxury DMV plot has been building to this moment of absurdly personalized government service.

jokes.text: The photo is so good that the DMV worker asks if Abbi is a model — gives her a modeling agent's card
jokes.setup: Abbi finally got the perfect photo after a lifetime of horrible ones
jokes.punchline: The photo is so good she gets talent-spotted at the DMV — the absurd over-delivery of the luxury DMV experience
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the luxury DMV premise. Being scouted by a modeling agent at the DMV is the logical extreme of a government office that has become a pampering experience. Abbi's 'I will' (after first hesitating) is a good character beat — she's earned this confidence.

jokes.text: Abbi tells a man 'you can call a man a bitch' — 'Later, bitch.'
jokes.setup: Abbi says 'later, bitch' to the DMV worker/modeling agent
jokes.punchline: She pauses to educate him that 'bitch' is gender-neutral before using it — a quick feminist footnote
jokes.explanation: The mini-lecture on gender-neutral 'bitch' usage before actually using it is a characteristically Broad City political commentary moment — feminist politics deployed casually in an exit line. Good economy.

jokes.text: Ilana has made herself business cards: 'Communications Liaison for Hillary Clinton, Future Presidentress of the United States'
jokes.setup: A staffer found a card on her desk with Ilana's name on it
jokes.punchline: Ilana made herself a title card with a made-up title ('Presidentress') and distributed it so people know how to reach her — despite being a volunteer sitting right there
jokes.explanation: 'Presidentress' is a great invented word — it feminizes 'president' in the same way 'actress' or 'waitress' does, which is both charming and slightly archaic. The title 'Communications Liaison' for an unpaid volunteer phone-caller is peak Ilana self-inflation. 'So everybody can be familiar with how to refer to me' delivered with complete seriousness is high character comedy. Very quotable. Strong earned score.

jokes.text: 'We know how to reach you, you're just right here.' / 'Fantastic, Barb. You do not go unnoticed. Know that.'
jokes.setup: Staffer points out the business cards are unnecessary since Ilana is physically present
jokes.punchline: Ilana responds to the deflation by complimenting the staffer — treating her reality check as worthy of recognition
jokes.explanation: 'You do not go unnoticed. Know that.' is a very quotable piece of Ilana generosity — she completely deflects the correction by handing out a compliment. It's disarming and reveals Ilana's actual warmth beneath the self-aggrandizement. The staffer's confused 'Okay' is the perfect reaction.

jokes.text: 'Riding me.' — Ilana's response to the staffer saying she's 'on top of her shit'
jokes.setup: Ilana completes the 'on top of your shit' / 'riding me' exchange with the staffer
jokes.punchline: She takes 'on top of her shit' as a sexual euphemism and completes the innuendo chain
jokes.explanation: Quick innuendo beat. The two-word response is economical. Not particularly original — Ilana's sexual innuendo reflex is well-established — but efficiently executed. The staffer presumably does not register this.

jokes.text: 'Hello? Yes, hello, gentle patriot. I'm calling on behalf of Ms. Hillary Rodham Clinton, first and only mother of Chelsea and Socks.'
jokes.setup: Ilana making campaign cold calls
jokes.punchline: 'Gentle patriot' as her opening — and identifying Hillary by her children AND her cat (Socks, the Clinton cat)
jokes.explanation: 'Gentle patriot' is a bizarre, charming opener that implies Ilana wrote her own script. Including Socks the cat alongside Chelsea as Hillary's defining relationship is an inspired specific detail — it's historically accurate (Socks was the Clinton White House cat) and very funny as a credential. High character specificity and quotability.

jokes.text: 'Sorry, no thanks, I'm at work.' — The voter hangs up on Ilana
jokes.setup: Ilana's enthusiastic 'gentle patriot' opening
jokes.punchline: The voter declines citing being at work — the call was received at the worst possible time and Ilana's pitch was apparently insufficient
jokes.explanation: Quick deflation beat after the elaborate 'gentle patriot' setup. The mundane excuse 'I'm at work' is funnier than a more dramatic rejection would be. Functional punchline.

jokes.text: 'Just to be clear, this is not a paid job. You're a volunteer.'
jokes.setup: Ilana just told her brother she's 'making money'
jokes.punchline: The staffer overhears and corrects the record immediately
jokes.explanation: The timing is everything — Ilana's big speech is immediately undercut by the staffer who clearly couldn't let the money claim stand. The specificity of 'just to be clear' implies this needed explicit clarification. Works perfectly as a capper to the previous joke (78). Both together are stronger than either alone.

jokes.text: Ilana's long silent pause after being told she's a volunteer, then '...Okay'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just been publicly corrected that she's not getting paid — in front of her brother
jokes.punchline: A beat of total silence before a defeated 'okay' — the pause is where the comedy lives
jokes.explanation: A silent reaction beat that relies entirely on Ilana Glazer's performance. The pause implies the processing of shame, reframing, and acceptance in real time. 'Okay' as the output of that processing is perfect — she has metabolized the humiliation in seconds and moved on. Economy is perfect: one word does all the work.

jokes.text: 'Oh! Ooof... I got sunscreen in my!' — Abbi at the second DMV visit, injuring herself again
jokes.setup: Abbi is arriving at the second, luxury online-appointment DMV
jokes.punchline: She injures herself in a new way (sunscreen in an unspecified eye/orifice) before even getting inside
jokes.explanation: Running gag continuation of Abbi's physical misfortune, but the sunscreen gag is a weaker version of the neck injury joke. The incomplete 'in my!' (presumably eye) is funny but the bit is running out of steam by this point. Competent but diminishing returns.

jokes.text: Ilana takes Abbi on a tour of the Clinton HQ as if it's a monument: 'She's worked in here. She's walked on these floors. She's... breathed this air.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is showing Abbi around the campaign office
jokes.punchline: She treats the mundane office as sacred ground because Hillary may have breathed the air — the relics are air molecules
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'worked in here' → 'walked on these floors' → 'breathed this air' is perfect — each relic is less tangible than the last until she reaches air itself. The reverence is Ilana at full Hillary-worship mode and it's very specific to her character. 'She's breathed this air' is the line — the absurdist peak of the escalation. Very quotable. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'That's my former desk.' / 'This is my former chair.' / 'This is the former leaning column. Sort of talk about big stuff that's going on, domestic, international. Very casual.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is touring Abbi through the office as if showing a historic site
jokes.punchline: The 'former leaning column' — she has a specific spot where she leaned for informal international policy chats, after one half-day of volunteering
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The escalation from desk → chair → 'former leaning column' is structurally excellent. A 'leaning column' as a named, cherished workspace is absurd enough on its own, but the addition of 'sort of talk about big stuff that's going on, domestic, international. Very casual.' turns it into a perfect comedy sentence. The specificity of 'domestic, international' implies Ilana has been having informal global policy discussions while leaning on a pillar after a few hours of volunteering. 'Very casual' is the perfect deadpan capper. Earns the high scores.

jokes.text: Ilana meets Hillary Clinton — her reaction is a series of disconnected words and hyperventilating
jokes.setup: Ilana has been worshipping Hillary from afar all day
jokes.punchline: When she actually meets her, she can only produce: 'Yas! Yas! Holy...'
jokes.explanation: The celebrity-meeting breakdown is a strong physical comedy beat. The joke is that Ilana, who has all the words all the time, is reduced to 'Yas' and hyperventilation. Glazer's physical performance carries this. High character integration — only Ilana would have this specific reaction. The actual cameo presumably drives high impact.

jokes.text: Ilana introduces herself to Hillary: 'Abbi. Hello. Proud demo. Crat. College! Aquarius. I pegged... Secretary Clinton.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is introducing herself to Hillary Clinton
jokes.punchline: The introduction fragments into non-sequiturs: Abbi's name first, then 'College!' as an accomplishment, zodiac sign, and the verb 'I pegged' dangling ambiguously before 'Secretary Clinton'
jokes.explanation: This is one of the two best jokes in the episode. The word salad introduction is perfectly constructed — each element is funnier than the last. Saying Abbi's name first (completely wrong). 'College!' as a credential with an exclamation. 'Aquarius' as relevant information. Then 'I pegged... Secretary Clinton' — the pause before 'Secretary Clinton' makes 'pegged' sound like something it isn't, which is entirely deliberate. The fragments reveal Ilana's brain completely malfunctioning from celebrity proximity. Every word is doing maximum work. Near-perfect scores are justified.
